figures.gloss: Týr (Tiwaz) — once the chief sky-god of the Germanic peoples, his very name the cognate of Dyēus.
figures.facet: The shard that faded: a sky-father demoted to a one-handed war-god — evidence of how a pantheon overwrites its own past.

figures.gloss: Thunder-god with the hammer Mjölnir; defender of gods and men against giants and the world-serpent.
Shapeshifting boundary-walker; companion and saboteur of the gods, father of monsters, the fuse of Ragnarök.
The shining god whose death darkens the world; he returns only after Ragnarök, to rule the world reborn.
figures.facet: The only resurrection that waits for the end — renewal deferred past the apocalypse itself.

figures.gloss: Half-living, half-corpse goddess who keeps those who die of sickness and old age.
Master-smith of legend — hamstrung and held captive, who forges out of his suffering a terrible revenge.
Queen of the Æsir, who knows the fate of all things and tells it to no one.
The world-ash whose roots and branches bind the nine worlds — the still center on which everything turns.

edges.note: Týr < Tīwaz < *Dyēus — the sky-father's name survived even as his rank did not.
Shared Indo-European thunderer pattern: hammer/axe, oak, and the serpent at the world's root.
The world-axis as cosmic tree / cosmic mountain — the still center binding the realms.
Two fire-and-boundary tricksters, mirror-opposite: Prometheus the punished benefactor, Loki the gift that curdles into catastrophe.
The cunning shapeshifter who wins by wit against stronger powers — trickster as destabiliser of the great.
Storm-and-war power who is the people's nearest protective god while the sky-father stays remote.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmic axis, but anchored deeply in the chthonic underground rather than reaching strictly upward into the heavens.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both function as brilliant, fire-associated trickster spirits whose disruptive influence is necessary to catalyze human and divine evolution.
Two feminine powers bound to death, but where Hel rules the dead in their place, Manat decides the MOMENT of dying — the underworld-queen and the fate-allotter as complementary faces of mortality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Shape-shifting, capricious agents of fate who undermine the established order without being defined by purely moral evil.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A boisterous, violent protector deity who battles national enemies from within the storm clouds.
Both from *Dyeus/*Tiwaz; Dievas kept the supreme sky-god role that Tyr surrendered to Odin and Thor.
Oak-and-thunder god wielding the bolt against a chthonic serpent — the same IE storm-template, distinct lineage from Perkūnas's *Perkwunos.
Goddesses presiding over birth, marriage and the measure of a life; Laima at the threshold rhymes with Frigg who knows all fates yet speaks none.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The liminal, supernaturally powerful smith figure whose mastery over metal renders him dangerous and distinct from normal society.
Both shards of a faded sky-father — Tyr demoted by sound-law cognate to Dyeus, Ortzi surviving only as grammar; the parallel is in the FADING, not in shared origin.
A storm-power crossing the sky with thunder and hail — but Eate is read as a portent to shelter from, not a champion-god to invoke.
The world-axis with heavens above and hells below — Sumeru and the world-tree as the same cosmological spine, separate families.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: While both are wily tricksters, Snilemun uses his cunning to gamble for humanity's survival against cosmic forces, whereas Loki drives cosmic destruction.
GOLD SEAM: Proud, robust storm-wielders who command thunder, wield iconic, culturally foundational axes or hammers, and champion structural order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A radiant figure whose return from a state of departure signals the rebirth of the world after a catastrophic age.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both embody the trickster's weaponization of rules and boundaries, adopting whatever shape or belief is operationally necessary to break consensus reality.
GOLD SEAM: A god of martial prowess whose primary domain is actually justice, covenants, and the lawful ordering of society.
GOLD SEAM: A cosmic tree upon which a divine figure hangs and suffers to gain ultimate victory and cosmic reconciliation.
GOLD SEAM: The beloved, innocent son of the divine whose tragic, unjust death shifts the cosmic age and causes the whole world to weep.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A shape-shifting, rule-breaking trickster whose subversion of societal norms is a catalyst for world-altering cosmic events.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: CAW uses the expanding rings of a growing tree as its structural metaphor for initiation, mirroring the World Tree's layered, organic cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmic tree inextricably bound to the physical suffering, bleeding, and ultimate preservation of a divine figure.
World-tree spanning upper and lower realms; the Ngaju tree's destruction-and-renewal parallels Yggdrasil's role as the axis bearing the cosmos.
The kin-trickster whose impatience and rebellion introduce death and disorder into a made world — the adversary-from-within across families.
The world ordered along a single descending axis lowered from heaven — granary-architecture beside world-tree as the frame that holds the cosmos.
Both are the sovereign queen-consort of the sky-king, tutelary mother of the people — a recurrent great-mother-beside-sky-father joint.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An immense cosmic tree whose roots and branches provide the structural framework for multiple worlds.
Hammer-and-thunder sky-gods who smite with a striking-tool — but Ukko is Uralic and Thor Indo-European (*þunraz); the shared weapon is convergence or areal influence, emphatically NOT a cognate.
Northern underworlds of cold and mist ruled by a death-keeping family; Tuonela and Hel rhyme as bleak realms of the departed across the Uralic/Germanic line, by neighbourhood not descent.

Some scholars read the Sampo as a world-pillar or sky-dome holding the cosmos, which would set it beside the world-axis like Yggdrasil — but the Sampo's nature is genuinely unsettled, so the seam is flagged speculative.
The world held together by a single coiling axis — serpent-as-world-frame beside tree-as-world-frame; a reach, flagged.
Axe/hammer-wielding thunderer of the sky — the storm-wielder joint across unrelated families.
GOLD SEAM: Master smiths and craftsmen whose unparalleled skill subjects them to betrayal and violence by those seeking to extract their secrets.

A reach: both order the totality of being into a single structure one ascends or descends, but Yggdrasil is a cosmic axis of nine worlds while the Pleroma is a fullness of emanated Aeons — structure of the All, by very different geometry. Flagged speculative.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are dangerous, monstrous, or captured entities that teach humanity secret metalworking and crafts.
GOLD SEAM: Macrocosmic trees that map the interconnected realms of existence and divine forces, ascended or descended by initiates in trance.
GOLD SEAM: The living, botanical tree functioning not merely as a sacred location, but as the literal, sheltering architecture of the divine.

The marrer-from-within the family of the makers — Flint the brother who roughens every good making, Loki the blood-brother of the gods whose mischief frays their order; the adversary as kin rather than external enemy, reached in two unconnected worlds.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Modern Odinism bridges the practitioner to the ancient mythic axis; Odin's ritual sacrifice on Yggdrasil serves as the ultimate template for shamanic initiation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Modern Heathenry directly revitalizes the historic Norse Thor, shifting him from a mythic relic to a living patron of the working class and community defense.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cosmic trees that serve as the structural framework of the universe, connecting divine, human, and underworld realms.

Both descend from the Indo-European thunder-deity complex (*Perkwunos / the dragon-slaying storm-wielder); Tarhunna's heart-and-eyes loss to Illuyanka rhymes with Thor's hammer-quest against the world-serpent.
The world-serpent as the storm-god's appointed adversary: Illuyanka and Jormungandr each embody the chaos-snake the thunderer must face, though Anatolia's serpent first prevails.
GOLD SEAM: The silent, massive, living anchor that passively bears the weight of the cosmic realms.

Both are thunder-powers and the people's near protector while the high creator stays remote — but Thor is a giant-fighting champion where Amadioha is a strict judge. A loose storm-wielder echo, flagged.

A reach: the cunning agent of chaos and reversal who is read as the cosmos's enemy — but Loki is woven into a genuine dualist eschatology (Ragnarök) where Ekwensu is a testing-force the Igbo never opposed to Chukwu. The parallel risks importing the very devil-frame the facet rejects; flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both embody the raw, unbridled energy of the thunderstorm, deeply associated with Thursday and localized, atmospheric power.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are powerful goddesses of female fertility and the dawn whose veneration resulted in their respective languages naming Friday in their honor.
Both arrange the cosmos vertically as worlds stacked on an axis — hells below, lived world at the middle, blessed at the top — though the Loka is a man's body where Yggdrasil is a tree.
GOLD SEAM: The mapping of the entire cosmos—both physical and spiritual—onto a singular, interconnected 'Tree of Life' structure.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A cosmic tree that centers the universe and acts as the gate between the earthly and divine realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An underworld conceptualized not as fire, but as a freezing, hydrologically northern realm of the dead.

A loose touch: both are bright, beloved figures bound to death and the hope of return, but Baldr dies once and waits beyond Ragnarök while Heitsi-Eibib rises again and again within the world. The shared note is dying-and-rising; the structures diverge sharply. Flagged.
The clever schemer forever undone by his own cunning, half-helper half-saboteur of the cosmic order; Iktómi and Loki are the trickster's two-faced intelligence in unrelated worlds.

Thunder-powers of the sky whose voice is the storm and whose ancient enemy is the great serpent/monster of the waters — the Thunderbirds against Uŋktéȟi as Thor against the World-Serpent; the storm-versus-serpent template reached far outside Indo-European.

Both name a great serpent of the deep set against the powers above, but loosely — Níðhöggr gnaws the world-tree's root where Uŋktéȟi wars with the sky; included as a faint cosmic-serpent-of-the-underneath echo only.
The axis that joins the worlds and gathers the directions — the upright pipe and centre-pole of the hoop answering the world-tree; vertical axis and quartered circle, the same cosmic centring.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The great sheltering world tree that serves as the central anchor of the cosmos and a focal point for gathering.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Storm gods who violently strike the heavens with an axe or hammer to produce thunder and lightning.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cunning tricksters who rely on illusion, shape-shifting, and sleight-of-hand to undo their more powerful adversaries.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The colossal cosmic tree that spans the heavens, the mortal plane, and the underworld, binding the universe together.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A chaotic, half-mortal son who disrupts the divine assembly and torments the established gods.
GOLD SEAM: The transformation of a functional, heavy tool into the paramount emblem of divine ordering power, though in Crete it is wielded exclusively by women.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Druidic Sacred Tree functionally mirrors Yggdrasil as the central cosmic pillar uniting the upper, middle, and lower realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The chaotic divine figure who is physically bound beneath the earth, whose movements manifest as earthquakes.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The legendary smith whose immense skill leads to his capture and mutilation/murder by a greedy, paranoid king.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The uncompromising female sovereign of the graveyard and the dead, operating outside the jurisdiction of the celestial pantheon.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an innocent, luminous figure of peace whose unjust death acts as a focal point for cosmological tragedy and eventual spiritual renewal.

Shape-shifting troublemakers whose disorder is woven into the cosmos itself — Coyote scattering the stars, Loki breeding chaos among the gods — the trickster as the mischief the world cannot do without.
Trickster whose appetite and deceit nonetheless drive cosmic events; the rogue inside the order rather than outside it.
The storm-power as a sky-warrior — but Thunderbird is the storm as living predator, not a god wielding a weapon. Functional storm-wielder parallel, divergent in form.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Explosive, weapon-wielding storm deities whose natural domain is thunder and striking, immediate justice.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both govern the grim, physical reality of the grave, the cemetery gates, and the ancestral dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The towering cosmic tree acting as an axis mundi, though the Asmat pole is ritually felled and inverted to transport souls rather than permanently standing.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The patron of chaos and discord who upends the artificial rules constructed by the ruling pantheon to inject necessary unpredictability.

Both atua/god of war and the founder of human martial order; Tū diverges by being also the literal ancestor of humankind, whose originary act is devouring his brothers' children to license the hunt — war and humanity in one figure.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A sovereign intimately tied to the literal graveyard and the physical reality of decay and ancestral bones.
GOLD SEAM: The Rosy Cross parallels the World Tree—a central, agonizing scaffold upon which divine knowledge is won and the soul blooms.

The strong gold here: 'Horagalles' is the Norse 'Þórr karl' (Thor the old man) borrowed by the Sámi from their Norse neighbours and laid over the native thunder-god — a documented name-borrowing across the Indo-European/Uralic line, the title imported even as Tiermes/Dierpmis stays indigenous.

An underworld of the dead ruled by a female sovereign — Jábmeáhkká and the Norse Hel both women who keep the departed; an analogous death-mistress across the Uralic/Germanic line, contact plausible but the parallel structural.
A loose touch: both are tricksters whose blundering is woven into the cosmic order, but Loki is no creator and ends as adversary, while |Kaggen is the world's own maker. Flagged as a stretch.
GOLD SEAM: Hyper-masculine lords of thunder, lightning, and justice, characterized by their immense appetites, fierce protection, and iconic weaponry.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmos understood as being structurally supported and physically manifested through a massive, sacred living tree.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ritual perfectly embodies the trickster's tactic of weaponizing the rules and taboos of the dominant culture against itself to force a chaotic shift.
GOLD SEAM: Both utilize shape-shifting—whether mythological or social—and psychological manipulation to outwit opponents and achieve personal aims.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A primordial tree serving as the central nervous system of the cosmos, connecting the physical earth to spiritual dimensions.
GOLD SEAM: A goddess presiding over a designated, sovereign realm of spirits and the dead, though Xiwangmu evolved to govern transcendent immortality rather than mere shade existence.

Odin's nine-night hanging on the world-tree to win wisdom rhymes hard with the shaman's ascent of the world-tree — and some scholars suspect real circumpolar diffusion behind the resemblance, though it stays unproven (tier 3).
World-tree at the cosmic centre joining the three worlds — one of the strongest North-Eurasian axis-mundi convergences; circumpolar diffusion is plausible but undemonstrated.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme consort who secures domestic, agricultural, and dynastic stability while the sky-father wages eternal war.
GOLD SEAM: The geometric, branching axis of the world around which cosmic stability and fertility are structured.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The great cosmic tree whose branches represent the protective structure of the universe, serving as the site of ultimate judicial assembly.
GOLD SEAM: Both function as the central axis that connects the realms of existence, though the Wiccan circle is temporarily generated rather than a permanent cosmic structure.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A massive tree connecting heavens, the human realm, and an underworld of dark forces.
Both bring death into a good creation, but Loki is a trickster-insider who turns, whereas Angra Mainyu is the adversary by original choice — chaos versus principled evil.

dossier.essence: The pre-Christian religion of the Norse and broader Germanic peoples of northern Europe, best attested for the Scandinavian Viking Age (c. 793–1100 CE) before Christianization. Polytheistic and non-doctrinal, it worshipped two divine families — the Æsir (Odin, Thor, Týr, Frigg) and the Vanir (Njord, Freyr, Freyja) — within a fatalistic cosmos moving toward its own destruction and rebirth at Ragnarök.
dossier.cosmos: The cosmos is organized around the world-ash Yggdrasil, which binds nine worlds including Asgard of the gods, Midgard of humans, Jotunheim of the giants, and Hel below; it was fashioned from the body of the primal giant Ymir, slain by Odin and his brothers. Fate — governed by the Norns and the concept of wyrd/urðr — overrides even the gods, who know they are doomed: at Ragnarök the world burns and the gods fall fighting the giants and monsters, after which a renewed earth rises and the innocent Baldr returns. The dead go variously to Odin's Valhalla (chosen slain warriors), Freyja's Folkvangr, or Hel (those dead of sickness and old age), among other destinations.
dossier.practice: Worship was decentralized and led by chieftains and kings rather than a separate priesthood: sacrifice (blót) of animals and feasting at seasonal festivals, offerings at halls, groves, and open-air sites, and rites at cult centers such as Uppsala (described by Adam of Bremen). Practices included sacral kingship, oath-taking on rings, seiðr (a sorcery-divination associated with Freyja and practiced largely by women), and burial with grave goods, including ship and mound burials. Belief expressed itself in poetry, honor, and fate more than in creed or congregation.
dossier.canon: An oral poetic tradition written down only after conversion, in Christian Iceland: the 'Poetic Edda' (Codex Regius, c. 1270), Snorri Sturluson's 'Prose Edda' (c. 1220), and skaldic verse and sagas — supplemented by earlier outside notices (Tacitus's 'Germania', 98 CE) and by archaeology.
dossier.reflects: Courage under doom — a religion that faces a cosmos fated to end, locating meaning in honor, kinship, and fate rather than in rescue from death or the final triumph of the gods.
sources: H. R. Ellis Davidson, 'Gods and Myths of Northern Europe' (1964)
John Lindow, 'Norse Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Heroes, Rituals, and Beliefs' (2001)
Neil Price, 'The Viking Way: Religion and War in Late Iron Age Scandinavia' (2002)
